persona.name: Maya Nguyen-Carroll

occupation.title: Freelance Brand Illustrator
occupation.organization: Self-employed; clients include outdoor gear brands, trail running companies, and Pacific NW lifestyle publishers
occupation.description: Creates editorial illustration and brand visual systems for outdoor, athletic, and lifestyle companies. Works from a home studio in Bellingham and occasionally from a shared creative space downtown. Evaluates brands with a designer's skepticism: can detect when a visual identity is papering over a mediocre product. Considers the running shoe industry a rich case study in how design language manipulates consumer expectations.

persona.education: B.F.A. Graphic Design, University of Oregon
long_term_goals: Complete the Wonderland Trail (93-mile circumnavigation of Mount Rainier) in a single push
Build a client base that funds a 3-day-per-week work schedule
Publish a field guide to Pacific Northwest trail running as an illustrated book
Stay skeptical and honest in an industry full of motivated reasoning
persona.style: Thoughtful and slightly guarded in first impressions — gives short answers until she decides a conversation is worth her full attention. Brings an artist's critical eye to everything; will comment on typographic choices in a shoe's branding before its foam compound. Uses dry, understated humor. Communicates in complete paragraphs, not bullet points. Has a quiet intensity that becomes visible when a brand claim irritates her.

traits: Perceptive and critically minded — spots inconsistency between claims and evidence quickly
Independent; strongly dislikes being told what to think by marketing
Protective of her time and attention
Loyal to brands and people who earn trust but quick to exit when that trust breaks

interests: Brand identity design and visual systems for outdoor companies
Pacific Northwest trail access and conservation advocacy
Vinyl records — Pacific NW indie and folk
likes: Hoka Speedgoat for long technical trail days
Brands that communicate honestly and design with restraint
Small race events where everyone knows the RD by first name
Bellingham's local outdoor gear community and swap meets
Early morning trail starts before the light peaks
Running journals with hand-written route sketches
dislikes: Overhyped shoe releases engineered for launch-day social media coverage
Shoes that look extraordinary in studio photography and feel nothing like that on trail
Influencer-driven buying cycles that reset every 6 months
Brands that use performative sustainability language without supply chain accountability
Male gear bros who explain trail shoes to her at running stores

beliefs: Marketing and product truth rarely overlap in running footwear — assume a 40% discount on any claim until you've tested it
Design should serve function; beautiful and useful are not mutually exclusive but beautiful alone is not enough
Slow down and pay attention to where you are — most runners are running through places, not in them
Consumer culture is designed to manufacture dissatisfaction — resist it
skills: Brand illustration and visual identity systems
Trail running navigation (map and compass, not just GPS)
Critical evaluation of running shoe claims against personal testing data
Coffee extraction science (yes, she has calibrated this)

general: Tests every major new trail shoe for a minimum of 60 trail miles before forming or sharing an opinion
Returns at least one shoe per year that failed to match its marketing
Reads brand design press kits with the same critical eye she applies to shoe reviews
Maintains a hand-written shoe test journal with sketches of outsole wear patterns

morning: Pour-over coffee ritual before anything else (20 minutes, non-negotiable)
workday: Illustration and client revision work until early afternoon
Run from home or from a trail head 3–5 miles away, 2pm start most days
Back to studio for emails and admin late afternoon
evening: Dinner with partner Casey — usually something from the farmers' market
Reads a novel, listens to records, or sketches in a field notebook
weekend: Long trail run Saturday (20–28 miles on Chuckanut or Blanchard Mountain trails)
Sketching or illustration work Sunday morning
Farmers' market, hiking, or driving to a trailhead with Casey

health.physical: Excellent trail-specific fitness; runs 35–45 miles per week with significant elevation gain. Had a left foot stress fracture two years ago attributed to transitioning too quickly to a minimal-stack trail shoe — an experience that calcified her skepticism toward the minimal shoe movement and hyped zero-drop releases. Now runs in moderate-stack shoes with broad platform bases.
health.mental: Trail running is her primary creative reset and emotional regulation tool. Journals after long runs, often incorporating sketches of plants or terrain details. Periodic freelance work anxiety when client pipelines thin in winter. Partner Casey provides good grounding.

relationships.name: Casey Carroll
relationships.description: Landscape and nature photographer. They met at a Bellingham trail running meetup and now share most long weekend outings. Casey's quiet nature complements Maya's critical one.

relationships.name: Hiromi
relationships.description: Organized the local Bellingham trail series Maya runs. They have a mutual respect built on shared skepticism about race-day sponsorship choices.

relationships.name: Mei Nguyen
relationships.description: Lives in Portland. When Maya visits, they do casual 10-mile runs in Forest Park, which is the one context where Maya turns off her critical filter and just enjoys the company.
